front: Under what constitutional standard is force used during an arrest, investigatory stop, or other seizure of a free person judged (Graham v. Connor)?
back: The Fourth Amendment's objective-reasonableness standard — not substantive due process.

front: Graham v. Connor: quote the holding on how excessive-force claims during a seizure must be analyzed.
back: "[A]ll claims that law enforcement officers have used excessive force ... in the course of an arrest, investigatory stop, or other 'seizure' of a free citizen should be analyzed under the Fourth Amendment and its 'reasonableness' standard, rather than under a 'substantive due process' approach."

front: Graham v. Connor: "The 'reasonableness' of a particular use of force must be judged from the perspective of {{c1::a reasonable officer on the scene}}, rather than with the {{c2::20/20 vision of hindsight}}."
back: a reasonable officer on the scene / the 20/20 vision of hindsight

front: Graham v. Connor: from whose perspective, and at what moment, is a use of force judged for reasonableness?
back: From the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene at the moment force is used — not with the 20/20 vision of hindsight.

front: Graham v. Connor: quote verbatim the three named factors that guide the reasonableness inquiry.
back: "[T]he severity of the crime at issue, whether the suspect poses an immediate threat to the safety of the officers or others, and whether he is actively resisting arrest or attempting to evade arrest by flight."

front: Graham v. Connor: what is the FIRST named reasonableness factor?
back: The severity of the crime at issue.

front: Graham v. Connor: what is the SECOND named reasonableness factor?
back: Whether the suspect poses an immediate threat to the safety of the officers or others.

front: Graham v. Connor: what is the THIRD named reasonableness factor?
back: Whether the suspect is actively resisting arrest or attempting to evade arrest by flight.

front: Graham v. Connor: what word in the opinion shows the three named factors are non-exclusive rather than a closed checklist, and what follows from it?
back: The word "including." Because the factors are non-exclusive, they are the starting point and force is judged on the totality of the circumstances.

front: Common pitfall (Graham): what is the error in judging force only against severity, threat, and resistance?
back: Treating the three factors as a closed test. Graham says "including" — they are illustrative; the standard is the totality of the circumstances, so you must account for the rest of the scene.

front: Graham v. Connor: what role does the officer's underlying intent or motivation play in the reasonableness inquiry?
back: None — the inquiry is purely objective: whether the actions are objectively reasonable in light of the facts confronting the officer, without regard to underlying intent or motivation.

front: Graham v. Connor: quote the line on how an officer's intentions affect (or don't affect) the analysis.
back: "An officer's evil intentions will not make a Fourth Amendment violation out of an objectively reasonable use of force; nor will an officer's good intentions make an objectively unreasonable use of force constitutional."

front: Common pitfall (Graham): why does a "good faith" or "malicious/sadistic" framing miss the mark on an excessive-force claim?
back: Because reasonableness is objective. Graham rejected the substantive-due-process good-faith test; an officer's subjective good or bad intent does not change whether the force was objectively reasonable.

front: Graham v. Connor: what allowance must the "calculus of reasonableness" make for the conditions officers face?
back: Allowance for the fact that officers are often forced to make split-second judgments in circumstances that are tense, uncertain, and rapidly evolving about the amount of force necessary.

front: What doctrine does Graham v. Connor anchor — why does it matter for any use-of-force question?
back: It establishes that using force during a seizure is itself a Fourth Amendment event, governing all such claims by a single objective-reasonableness standard rather than substantive due process.

front: Graham v. Connor arose as a civil 42 U.S.C. § 1983 suit. For a suppression-focused analysis, what does it supply and what stays out of scope?
back: It supplies the governing Fourth Amendment force standard (objective reasonableness). Whether an officer is personally liable — qualified immunity and § 1983 damages — is a separate civil question and does not change what the Fourth Amendment requires.

front: Tennessee v. Garner: when may deadly force be used against a fleeing suspect?
back: Only where it is necessary to prevent escape and the officer has probable cause to believe the suspect poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury to the officer or others.

front: Tennessee v. Garner: quote the rule limiting deadly force against a fleeing suspect.
back: "[S]uch force may not be used unless it is necessary to prevent the escape and the officer has probable cause to believe that the suspect poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury to the officer or others."

front: Tennessee v. Garner: "[A]pprehension by the use of {{c1::deadly force}} is a {{c2::seizure}} subject to the reasonableness requirement of the Fourth Amendment."
back: deadly force / seizure

front: Which case held that shooting an apparently unarmed, non-dangerous fleeing suspect to stop the escape was an unreasonable seizure?
back: Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S. 1 (1985).

front: Scott v. Harris: how did the Court characterize Garner's relationship to Graham's reasonableness standard?
back: Garner is not a rigid separate test; it is "simply an application" of Graham's Fourth Amendment reasonableness standard to a particular type of force.

front: Scott v. Harris: quote the "on/off switch" passage on Garner.
back: "Garner did not establish a magical on/off switch that triggers rigid preconditions whenever an officer's actions constitute 'deadly force.' Garner was simply an application of the Fourth Amendment's 'reasonableness' test ... to the use of a particular type of force."

front: Scott v. Harris: how did the Court rule on the officer's decision to ram a fleeing motorist whose reckless high-speed flight endangered the public?
back: It was reasonable — the Court had "little difficulty in concluding it was reasonable" for the officer to take the action he did.

front: Common pitfall: what is wrong with reading Garner as a rigid two-part precondition for any use of deadly force?
back: Scott v. Harris corrects it — there is no magical on/off switch. Garner is Graham reasonableness applied to a non-dangerous fleeing suspect; a dangerous fleeing suspect (e.g., a reckless high-speed driver) is a different totality, so reasonableness still turns on the totality of the circumstances.

front: Common pitfall: how do the Fourth Amendment force standard and officer liability differ?
back: Graham fixes the constitutional force standard (objective reasonableness). Qualified immunity and § 1983 damages are separate civil questions and do not change what the Fourth Amendment requires.
